text: The patient is positioned supine on the operating table. The surgical team uses a four-trocar technique, with two 10mm trocars placed at the umbilicus and right lower quadrant, and two 5mm trocars positioned at the left flank and upper umbilical midline.
The surgeon identifies and ligates the lower mesenteric vessels near the trice angle during the laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon performs dissection anteriorly to the durotus fascia, progressing towards the pancreas and spleen, carefully separating tissue layers in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the splenic flexure by opening the lesser sac and parietocolic ligaments, revealing the anterior aspect of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the splenic flexure of the colon and begins dissecting a giant cyst closely adjacent to the bladder wall, requiring careful manipulation due to its intimate proximity.
The surgeon identifies an opening in the bladder wall adjacent to the sigmoid colon and sutures it using 4-0 PDS sutures.
The surgeon tests the suture for leakage by injecting methylene blue dye, observing no leakage at the site.
The surgeon performs a colonic section followed by a colorectal anastomosis, using a circular stapler to create a terminoterminal anastomosis.
